title: The Open-Source Revolution
concept: Open-source projects (like LLaMA, Mistral, Gemma) scaled weights down to local sizes (8B-70B), allowing developers to build custom models affordably.

exercises.prompt: What does it mean when an AI model is described as 'open-source'?

options.text: They keep their code and models secret behind a paid wall.
They make their code and model weights free for the public to use and customize.
They are only allowed to be used by government researchers.

exercises.explain: Open-source models are famous for sharing their internal files and parameters publicly so anyone can build on them.

cards.title: AI in Your Pocket
cards.body: Have you ever wanted to build your own AI app, but worried about running out of money to pay for expensive cloud services? The open-source revolution has changed the game by bringing powerful, scaled-down AI directly to your personal device!

cards.title: What is Open-Source AI?
cards.body: Open-source AI refers to models whose internal 'weights' (the trained knowledge and connections) are shared freely with the public. Instead of sending data to a giant company's cloud server, you can download the model itself (often scaled down to compact sizes like 8 Billion parameters) and run it on your own computer.

cards.title: The Master Recipe Book
cards.body: Imagine the AI world as a restaurant industry. For a long time, the best dishes were served in exclusive, high-end restaurants behind locked kitchen doors (closed-source APIs). Open-source models are like master chefs publishing their secret recipes online. Now, anyone can bake a delicious 5-star cake right in their own kitchen using their own ingredients!

cards.title: AI on Your Laptop
cards.body: Companies like Meta (with LLaMA) and Google (with Gemma) have released smaller, highly optimized models. Instead of needing thousands of industrial servers, a developer can run a 7B or 8B parameter model on a single high-end laptop to power an offline coding assistant or an interactive game character.

cards.title: Myth: Bigger is Always Better
cards.body: Many people believe that smaller, local models (like 8B or 7B parameters) are too weak to be useful compared to giant cloud models. In reality, these compact models are incredibly smart because they are trained on high-quality data, making them perfect for specific, everyday tasks at a fraction of the cost.

cards.title: Cloud vs. Local AI
cards.body: Let's look at the difference between using closed-source APIs and running open-source models locally.

right.text: The model is downloaded directly to your device. You run it for free, customize it, and keep all your data private.

left.text: Data is sent to a giant external cloud server. You pay per request, and you cannot see or edit the model's inner workings.

exercises.prompt: Why do open-source AI projects scale down their models to sizes like 8 Billion (8B) parameters?

options.text: To make them run slower on purpose.
To allow them to run locally on affordable developer hardware.
To prevent them from understanding complex human languages.
To force developers to buy expensive supercomputers.

exercises.explain: Local models are scaled down (typically 8B to 70B parameters) so they can fit into the memory of consumer or smaller developer hardware.

exercises.prompt: True or False: Open-source AI models require an active, paid internet connection to a cloud provider to function.
exercises.explain: Open-source models like LLaMA and Gemma can be downloaded and run entirely offline on your own machine.

exercises.prompt: Fill in the blank to complete the statement about open-source models:
exercises.sentence: By sharing their model [blank], open-source projects allow developers to run AI on their own hardware.
exercises.explain: Open-source AI models make their trained 'weights' available for anyone to download, modify, and run locally.

exercises.prompt: Identify which scenario represents using an open-source AI workflow:

options.text: A developer downloads a Gemma 8B model and fine-tunes it on their own computer for free.
A developer pays a monthly subscription fee to use ChatGPT via a web browser.
A company sends all user prompts to a closed third-party cloud API.

exercises.explain: The first option describes a local, customizable open-source workflow. The others describe closed, proprietary API setups.

exercises.prompt: Why has the open-source revolution (like Gemma and LLaMA) been such a game-changer for independent software developers?

options.text: They allow developers to run, customize, and own the models locally without relying on expensive cloud APIs.
They only run on supercomputers and cannot be customized by normal developers.
They require paying a monthly subscription fee to the original creator for every prompt.
They are completely locked and do not allow developers to see how they work.

exercises.explain: Open-source models like LLaMA and Gemma bring the core brains of AI (weights) directly to developers, freeing them from relying on expensive, cloud-hosted APIs for every single request.
